news.title: La Liga boss says FIFA is 'destroying football' and calls on Infantino to quit
news.lede: La Liga president Javier Tebas said FIFA president Gianni Infantino should resign, accusing the governing body of "destroying the football industry" by expanding international competitions at the expense of domestic leagues and clubs. Tebas has repeatedly criticized FIFA over fixture congestion and plans to further expand the World Cup. In an interview with Italian newspaper La Gazzetta dello Sport, when Tebas was asked if Infantino should quit, he said: "In my opinion, yes, I think his time is up." Infantino said in April that he would seek re-election for a fourth term as FIFA president, with the vote for the 2027-2031 cycle scheduled to take place in Morocco on March 18 next year.

news.title: In the US, the 2026 World Cup’s legacy will be an invitation to do it all again | Leander Schaerlaeckens
news.lede: Fifa’s gargantuan revenue influx from the tournament, with little left over for locals, will beget more exploitation. You do not, under any circumstance, have to hand it to Fifa. You do, however, have to acknowledge that the 2026 World Cup was successful in all of the ways it was intended to be successful. The soccer was interesting and competitive and dramatic, albeit mostly not great – not that this really a necessary ingredient for a compelling sporting product.

news.title: Nico Williams, Ferran Torres and Spain’s adaptable World Cup excellence | Jeff Rueter
news.lede: The two substitutes combined on the World Cup winning goal not by changing the game, but by offering it what it needed, when it was needed. As Spain built into the second half of extra-time of Sunday’s World Cup final, it was already clear that Argentina hoped to make them suffer. Every challenge left no stud unshown. Their defenders labored to block striker Mikel Oyarzabal from his typical lanes of service, with Spain taking 11 of their 20 attempts from beyond the box.

news.title: ‘The scrutiny is constant’: referee Anthony Taylor retires from football
news.lede: 47-year-old took charge of two tournament matches. Taylor has refereed in the Premier League for 16 years. Anthony Taylor has announced his retirement from elite refereeing after a distinguished career spanning more than two decades and 831 matches. One of England’s most accomplished officials, the 47-year-old officiated 668 matches in the professional game in England.

news.title: Here are 48 reasons to cherish the World Cup (minus the Geopolitics)
news.lede: The Geopolitics World Cup is already fading from view, replaced by a thin July gruel of pre-season friendlies and transfer tales. But there’s still time to cast another look back over the 48-team, 104-game, 308-goal leviathan that has dominated the last six weeks of our lives. Without further ado, here are 48 reasons to cherish this year’s World Cup (minus the Geopolitics). The very first game between Mexico and South Africa saw three red cards dished out, ITV showing off their studio’s Manhattan backdrop, and a duck anointed as a national hero.

news.title: Argentina World Cup Final red card rescinded, face potential FIFA disciplinary action
news.lede: FIFA has opened an investigation into potential disciplinary violations by Argentina following their World Cup Final loss to Spain. The global governing body of soccer has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to review the incidents that occurred after Sunday's match at MetLife Stadium. Multiple Argentina players and coaching staff members could now face serious sanctions if found guilty, while the Argentine Football Association (AFA) also risks another monetary penalty. A statement from FIFA confirmed: "Following an assessment of the relevant match reports for the FIFA World Cup final between Spain and Argentina, and in line with article 36 of the FIFA disciplinary code (FDC), the FIFA disciplinary committee has appointed a disciplinary and ethics prosecutor to investigate potential breaches of the FDC with regard to the post-match incidents.

news.title: Neuer to Neymar: 11 World Cup players who retired from international football
news.lede: Germany’s 2014 World Cup winning goalkeeper and a Brazilian icon are among those who have have called time after the 2026 tournament. First participated in the World Cup in 2010 before winning the trophy four years later. Germany have had little success since and their penalty shootout defeat by Paraguay marked the end of Neuer’s international career. He initially retired after Euro 2024 but reversed that decision a few weeks before this tournament.

news.title: Tebas goes all in: "FIFA is destroying football, Infantino must go"
news.lede: Javier Tebas returned to Madrid from New York after watching the second World Cup of the Spanish national team. The president of LaLiga returns happy with the victory but very angry with the role of FIFA throughout the tournament. '"FIFA organizes things as it pleases, for its own interests, not for football. So if they need a 27-minute break, they do it.
